markdown.markdown:
I spend a lot of time in git. These aliases save me keystrokes every single day. Add them to your `~/.gitconfig` under `[alias]`:
## The Aliases
```plain text
[alias]
  co = checkout
  sw = switch
  br = branch
  st = status -sb
  lg = log --oneline --graph --decorate -20
  amend = commit --amend --no-edit
  wip = !git add -A && git commit -m "wip"
  undo = reset HEAD~1 --mixed
```
## What They Do
### `git co` / `git sw`
Shorthand for checkout and switch. I use `sw` for branches and `co` for files:
```bash
git sw feature-branch
git co -- src/file.ts
```
### `git st`
Short status with branch info. Compact and readable:
```bash
$ git st
## main...origin/main
 M src/app.ts
?? src/new-file.ts
```
### `git lg`
A compact, visual log. Shows the last 20 commits with a graph:
```bash
$ git lg
* a1b2c3d (HEAD -> main) fix: resolve login bug
* d4e5f6a feat: add user dashboard
* g7h8i9j refactor: extract auth module
```
### `git amend`
Add staged changes to the last commit without changing the message. Perfect for "oops, forgot a file":
```bash
git add forgotten-file.ts
git amend
```
### `git wip`
Stage everything and commit as "wip". Great for saving work before switching branches:
```bash
git wip
git sw other-branch
# ... do work ...
git sw -
git undo  # unwrap the wip commit
```
### `git undo`
Undo the last commit but keep the changes staged. Pairs perfectly with `git wip`.
---
Small optimizations compound. These six aliases probably save me 15 minutes a day — and more importantly, they reduce friction so I stay in flow.
